content.full_text:
THE CALIFORNIA CHANNEL

January 31, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814-0814

To Whom It May Concern:

I am writing to seek the Fair Political Practices Commission's advice on
the need to file a Lobbyist Certification Statement and a Lobbyist Employers
Registration Statement on behalf of the California Channel, a nonprofit tax
exempt educational corporation.

The concept for the California Channel, a proposed statewide public affairs
television channel, evolved from a two year feasibility report conducted at
the USC Annenberg School of Communications. The exhaustive study
demonstrates the lack of state legislative media coverage, studies media
coverage in other states and countries and describes, in detail, the
necessary steps involved in building a public affairs television network
similar to C-SPAN's coverage of Congressional proceedings.

The 325 page academic report recommends that the Legislature install and
operate remote controlled cameras to televise Assembly and Senate floor
proceedings, committee hearings and press conferences. The video signals
would be distributed throughout the Capitol allowing legislators, staff,
lobbyists, the press and visitors to the Capitol to view legislative proceedings
on television sets in offices and designated viewing areas.

The video signals would also be available to all media organizations,
including the California Channel which would distribute programming
around the state to cable television systems, educational institutions and
public television stations. The California Channel would be funded by
foundation grants, corporate support and cable television subscriber fees.

Recently, we began the process of meeting with legislators to discuss the
report's recommendations and seek their support for the concept of
televising legislative sessions. The Senate and Assembly have both held
informational hearings on the issue. However, there has been no specific
legislation or resolution introduced. Even if legislation was introduced and
passed, there would be no guarantee that the California Channel would be
given access to the Legislature's video signal.

10951 W. PICO BLVD, THIRD FLOOR, LOS ANGELES, CA 90064 • (213) 475-1015 • FAX (213) 474-7083

In a short period, we may meet the contacts test. Do we qualify as a lobbyist and lobbyist employer? We believe we may not qualify on two grounds:

(a) No Pending Legislation or Resolution: The California Channel report is an academic document. While the report urges the legislature to install cameras, there is no pending legislation or legislative resolution. Our discussions with legislators involve an explanation of the California Channel report and a discussion on how a public affairs television network would work.

(b) Media Exemption: The proposed California Channel would be a noncommercial television network that, as a member of the press and in its course of business, wants to televise legislative proceedings. Do members of the electronic media have to register as lobbyists when they ask for legislative permission to bring in cameras to televise floor sessions and committee hearings? For example, if a broadcast organization wanted the Legislature to install a special conduit that would allow television signals to be transported from the Capitol to the front lawn, would it have to register as a lobbyist? In some cases, over 25 contacts with legislators and staff members would need to be made before the necessary permission (an administrative action) is granted.

Your advice on this matter would be greatly appreciated. If you should have any further questions, please do not hesitate to call.

With best regards,

[Signature]
Paul Koplin
Executive Director

PUBLIC STRIKES
New column: Reviews

GOLDEN STATE REPORT

The magazine of politics and public policy in California

How safe is it?

Cal-SPAN?

A non-profit group, armed with a new study, thinks that giving the Capitol
its own television channel would boost democracy. But skeptics say it
would harm a private satellite service and draw few viewers.

By STEVE SWART

Picture this: It's been a long and tiring day at the office. You have just successfully negotiated the Santa Monica and San Diego freeways during the afternoon crunch time. You arrive home about 6:45 p.m., kick off your shoes, settle into your favorite easy chair and turn on the television.

With your trusty remote control, you search for a little audio-visual diversion. Zap. Dan Rather. Zap. Tom Brokaw. Zap. Zap. "Hogan's Heroes" and "He-man." Zap. The state Assembly, on tape delay, debating the merits of the governor's proposed budget.

Or how about a 45-minute news conference with Gov. George Deukmejian? Or a Senate Elections and Reapportionment Committee voting to move California's presidential primary to mid-March?

Ratings notwithstanding, there's a move afoot to institute all-out, exhaust-

Steve Swart is the Capitol correspondent for KCRA (Channel 3) in Sacramento.

tive coverage of the state Capitol. A coalition of educators, business leaders, public interest groups and broadcasters insist it's an idea long overdue. And some of the Legislature's key leaders, upset at the lack of commercial television coverage of the Capitol, are listening.

"Our polls and focus groups indicate that the public is generally dissatisfied with the adequacy of their current sources of news of state government," says Tracy Westen, an assistant professor at the Annenberg School of Communications, University of Southern California.

Westen as project director and coauthor of a lengthy and well-publicized study of California's electronic media coverage of Sacramento. It was less than flattering. In fact, it was downright scathing in its analysis, concluding: "Californians learn shockingly little of their state government from the news media. Those who are substantially dependent on local television or radio for daily information live in

virtual ignorance about state political affairs."

Broadcasters themselves are the first to concede their inadequacies. "I don't think there's any question that — given all the constraints in format, time and expense — we've allowed ourselves to get away from that kind of coverage," says Vic Biondi, executive director of the California Broadcasters Association. "And it's not just politics. We probably don't cover as much substantial news in the community as we should. We could do better."

Proposing to fill the void is a non-profit group called the California Channel, which is trying to convince the Legislature that California's lawmakers deserve to be on television, statewide, perhaps 12 hours a day.

Westen is one of the California Channel's board members.

The California Channel proposes that it be allowed to provide viewers with direct access to the stations of

JANUARY 1990    33

their elected officials. It has modeled
its effort after C-SPAN, which has
been televising congressional proceed­
ings for 10 years and now boasts a
national audience approaching 22
million. Currently, about 5 million
California households are wired for
cable television.
The California Channel would tel­
evise Sacramento political happenings
during a projected four-, eight- or 12-
hour broadcast day. It would offer a
combination of live and taped events,
plus roundtable discussions, even live
audience participation phone-in shows.
Many legislators are intrigued with
the idea of expanded coverage. "Ex­
posure can only be good for this
place," says Assemblyman Richard
Katz (D-Sepulveda). Other legislators
on both sides of the aisle seem to agree.
Assemblyman Bruce Bronzan (D-
Fresno) says there could be abuses and
"grandstanding" by some lawmakers,
but "the more light you turn on (the
Legislature), the more people are able
to see government work." Assembly­
man Tim Leslie (R-Citrus Heights)
calls it a "good idea" that likely will
improve decorum on the floor of the
Assembly.
Heaven knows, the Assembly could
use a dose of decorum, but there's cer­
tainly no guarantee that the presence
of television cameras will do the trick.
Earlier this year, cameras caught the
Assembly's most irascible and outspoken member, Dick Floyd (D-Haw­
thorne), going toe-to-toe with Gil
Ferguson (R-Orange County) during
debate on Floyd's mandatory helmet
bill for motorcyclists.
Ferguson: "Now you're going to tell
us what to do with our heads."
Floyd: "We don't have to tell Mr.
Ferguson that. We know where his
head is — full-time where the sun
doesn't shine."
Then, again, such salty language
from our elected representatives just
might help the ratings.
San Francisco Sen. Quentin Kopp
(Ind.-San Francisco), who has been
known for his oratorical theatrics,
seems exuberant at the thought of
televising the Legislature. "It'll be a
force to compel legislators to be on bet­
ter behavior, to present themselves
with more grace and consummate
courtesy toward their fellow legisla­
tors.
"I think it'll increase the quality of
debate," he says, "and I think it'll have

a therapeutic effect on the Legislature."
Kopp pauses a second, then finishes
off his argument: "And it'll improve
the quality of dress, too."
Taking the larger view is Bob Con­
nelly, the Assembly's chief administra­

Entrepreneur
Steve Mallory

Advocate
Tracy Westen

tive officer. "The broader the base of
active participation in the legislative
process, the less there's going to be this
feeling that the Legislature is remote
from the principal interests of the peo­
ple," Connelly says.
Only days after the proposal was
made, the "Cal-SPAN" concept drew
rave reviews from California newspa­
pers. "It's plainly unhealthy to have
so much important state business con­
ducted out of sight and out of mind,"
intoned the Los Angeles Daily News.
"A TV eye under the Capitol might
hold legislators more accountable,"
the San Francisco Examiner editoria­
lized, "and it also might provide
triage for the Legislature's bush­
whacked image."

But it doesn't take a programming
genius to figure out that televising
the Legislature could be fraught with
pitfalls. For every moment of high
drama, there are hours upon hours of
tedium. Floor sessions rarely start on
time; often they're 45 minutes to an
hour late.
Closed caucuses, called at a mo­
ment's notice, can last for more than
an hour. A bill can be designated a
"special order" for a 10 a.m. debate
and not be taken up until 11:30 a.m. or
later — or not at all. That's a lot of
dead airtime, and television demands
tight timetables.
Beyond that, many of the thousands
of bills are innocuous and devoid of
interest to the average Californian.
Will viewers stay tuned to hear an As­
sembly floor debate on AB 564, for

example, which standardizes the
contents of goat milk products?
Or will they flip those dials to watch
lawmakers discuss AB 761, which re­
quires the Department of Boating and
Waterways to develop a regional atlas
of shoreline erosion for the Southern
Orange-San Diego counties area?
"Many of the issues are irrelevant
to a vast majority of Californians,"
says Assemblyman John Burton (D-
San Francisco). "I don't know whether
it would make sense or not."
Westen, of course, disagrees. "A
substantial majority (of Californians)
indicate that they would watch some of
the Capitol programs regularly," he
cites a survey taken in late 1987 by the
San Francisco State University Public
Research Institute, in which 89 percent
of the respondents expressed at least
minimal interest in watching Capitol
coverage on a public information
channel.
In its presentation, the California
Channel has the program day mapped
out to the minute: live floor debates
and committee hearings, live press
conferences, talk shows and taped
replays.
Both the Senate and the Assembly
have begun hearings on televising
legislative proceedings, but they're
marching to the beat of different drum­
mers. "We're exploring the sensibili­
ties of going it alone," according to
one Assembly source, perhaps as early
as mid-1990.
Assembly Speaker Willie Brown has
established a special Assembly task
force to explore TV coverage of the
Legislature and has said he would like
to begin gavel-to-gavel coverage.
Brown believes "the public would ap­
preciate an unfiltered view of what
decisions are made by their elected
representatives and how they are
made."
Never mind that many of those de­
cisions are made well out of camera
range, in closed caucuses or informal
settings. During the 1989 legislative
session, leaders arrived at some of
their most important decisions inside
the governor's office, well out of public
earshot.
Under the proposal devised by the
California Channel — one of several
floating around the Capitol — the Leg­
islature would install and operate re­
mote-controlled cameras to televise
the floor debates, committee hearings
and press conferences. The California

34 GOLDEN STATE REPORT

Would expanded TV coverage of the Capitol benefit or bore viewers?

Channel would distribute the coverage statewide by satellite to cable systems, public broadcast stations, commercial news media and educational institutions for use as they see fit.

Some critics, however, question the wisdom of the Legislature controlling the cameras. Will lawmakers decide to restrict radio and television reporters and technical crews from floor sessions and committee hearings once gavel-to-gavel feeds are implemented? Will they turn altruistic "good government" concept into a self-serving political device?

Ed Allen, co-founder of C-SPAN, says "there's a certain amount of manipulation of the media. The main thing, though, is that it's truly unearthed, great-to-gavel coverage. Realistically, however, true gavel-to-gavel coverage of the Legislature is impossible. On any given day, for example, a half-dozen committees might meet simultaneously. Judgments would have to be made as to which committee hearing should be televised. Legislators given programming carte blanche can influence public opinion on a volatile issue or help a colleague who needs a little added exposure during an election year.

Technically and financially, wiring the state Capitol for pictures and sound is a snap. Currently, legislators, lobbyists and news organizations receive live audio feeds of Capitol hearings and news conferences. Assembly chief Connelly believes the existing wiring for the computer systems could be used to support the cable signal.

"If you want to generate a signal from the Assembly and Senate chambers, then you'd have to make some modifications, but it's minimal," Connelly says. "Generating a signal from the committee rooms would be a wiring problem, but it's not insurmountable."

Connelly says the Assembly, at least, is likely to look at an internal system first, allowing each Assembly member to pick up feeds of ongoing committee hearings.

Westen estimates that the cost of installing the equipment in the state Capitol would range from $850,000 to $2 million, with annual operating costs of less than $1 million.

Interestingly, the California Channel Media study and proposal come at a time when many California television stations appear to be increasing, not decreasing, their coverage of state and governmental affairs.

True, no out-of-town television station maintains a Capitol bureau anymore, but a former NBC news correspondent has stepped in to fill the void. Steve Mallory created Northern California Satellite in 1987. The service feeds daily stories and interviews from Sacramento to 15 television stations. From San Diego to Medford, Ore.—including stations in the huge Los Angeles market—viewers are getting at least a taste of what their elected representatives are up to.

Mallory, whose investors have sunk nearly $1 million into the satellite news venture, says expanded coverage of the Capitol is "a terrific, healthy idea, something that Californians need without a doubt."

But he's concerned, quite naturally, that if a non-profit organization receives the blessing of the Legislature for an exclusive audio-video franchise, he'll be out of business. "I'm opposed to taxpayers' dollars supporting this," he says. "I do it for profit. What they're proposing is what I do.

"Along come these non-profits (looking) for tax breaks," he continues. "They are educators, teachers, public relations people, corporations and special interest groups (with) no experience in journalism."

Mallory also takes exception to Westen's contention that television has abandoned the Capitol and ignored state politics. "The Capitol is being

JANUARY 1990 35

covered more than ever," he says.
Biondi, representing the broad-
casters, echoes those words. "I object
to Tracy Weston and the people polish-
ing their own apple by dumping on us.
We're willing to take our lumps. But
there's no connection between that or-
ganization's capability to put that chan-
nel together and why we don't do it."
Contrary to the California Chan-
nel's ambitious plan of government
programming, Biondi proposes that
audio and video sources simply be fed
outside the Capitol building — un-
edited and in their entirety — to
television stations, cable facilities, col-
leges and universities. A C-SPAN type
of operation would be merely one of
several possible uses of this enhanced
coverage of the Capitol, Biondi says.
He suggests that neither the Legis-
lature nor the governor have any in-
volvement, financially or editorially,
in the condition of the feed or its uses.
The California Channel is perfectly
willing to have the Legislature pick up
part of the tab for start-up and annual
operating costs. It says it would adopt
guidelines to ensure that its coverage
is impartial and balanced.
But a Senate source, who asked not

to be identified, suggests it would be
difficult to prevent lawmakers from
meddling in such programs. "If we're
paying for it, shouldn't we have a voice
in it? After all, a controlled TV helps
them come re-election time."

Should taxpayers pay for this in the
first place? "I would be concerned
about getting taxpayer money to front
this," Assemblyman Katz says. "If
they can come up with a foundation
first, then it makes sense to do."

There's doubt that television is
an accepted — and basically un-
obtrusive — participant on the floors
of the Legislature. Younger legislators
who grew up in the television age tend
to understand the medium, its limita-
tions and prospects for political
manipulation.
Older lawmakers, however, may be
enchanted with television but fear what
it could show.
Senate Republican Caucus Chair-
man John Doolittle, who has used tele-
vision effectively to enhance his
career, nonetheless takes a jaundiced
view of televised floor sessions. Not-
ing that Senate sessions tend to be de-
liberative and sedate, he suggests that

the presence of cameras will cause
members to "posture and pander."
In the Assembly, where debates tend
to be raucous, at times profane and in-
terminably long, with or without the
cameras, Doolittle thinks gavel-to-
gavel coverage might force some mem-
bers to clean up their act. "It'll affect
each house differently," the senator
says. "Ours will get worse. Theirs will
get better."

But no less an expert than veteran
political consultant Richie Ross con-
tends televising the Legislature will
have no impact whatsoever. Ross has
managed several legislative campaigns
and currently is masterminding Atty.
Gen. John Van de Kamp's run for gov-
ernor. He scoffs at surveys indicating
a public appetite for Capitol news.
"This (Sacramento) is the seat of
state government, with more state em-
ployees and all the Capitol employ-
ees," Ross says. "But people who
work in the Capitol never watched
'Sacramento Week in Review.'
And, he insists, they're not going to
watch televised proceedings from the
state Capitol. "It's boring and irrele-
vant. I don't think anyone is going to
watch it. I wouldn't watch it."

Where do
legislators go
when they
leave office?

HYATT REGENCY SACRAMENTO
AT CAPITOL PARK

1209 L STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 USA
916 443 1234

36 GOLDEN STATE REPORT

Our discussions with legislators involve an explanation of the California Channel report and a discussion on how a public affairs television network would work. (b) Media Exemption: The proposed California Channel would be a noncommercial television network that, as a member of the press and in its course of business, wants to televise legislative proceedings. Do members of the electronic media have to register as lobbyists when they ask for legislative permission to bring in cameras to televise floor sessions and committee hearings?
sections.question_synthetic: Must the California Channel register as a lobbyist and lobbyist employer when meeting with legislators to discuss televising legislative proceedings, or does a media exemption apply to nonprofit educational television networks seeking access to film legislative sessions?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. Document includes substantial background material (magazine article) that provides context but is not part of the formal advice letter request. No formal CONCLUSION section exists in this document. The extraction confidence is moderate due to the inclusion of extraneous material and informal presentation.

embedding.summary: The California Channel, a nonprofit tax-exempt educational corporation, seeks FPPC advice on whether it must register as a lobbyist and lobbyist employer when meeting with legislators to discuss a proposal to televise legislative proceedings. The organization argues it may not qualify based on the lack of pending legislation and potential media exemption status.
